1: Chapter 28 stages the most dramatic prophetic confrontation in the book: the clash between Jeremiah and Hananiah in the Temple. In that same year, at the beginning of the reign of Zedekiah king of Judah, in the fifth month of the fourth year, Hananiah the son of Azzur, the prophet from Gibeon, spoke to me in the house of YHWH. The date is approximately 594 BC — the same year as Jeremiah's yoke sign-act (chapter 27). Hananiah is from Gibeon (a Levitical city, Joshua 21:17), has prophetic credentials, and speaks in the Temple — all marks of institutional legitimacy. He is not presented as a charlatan from the fringes but as an established prophetic figure.
2: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon. Hananiah uses the authoritative prophetic formula (thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel) — the same formula Jeremiah uses. He claims divine authorization and invokes the same divine title, making his message externally indistinguishable from Jeremiah's. The claim itself — I have broken the yoke of Babylon — directly contradicts Jeremiah's yoke-sign-act of chapter 27. Two prophets, same formula, opposite messages: this is the crisis of discernment that chapter 28 forces the reader to navigate.
3: Within two years I will bring back to this place all the vessels of YHWH's house, which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon took away from this place and carried to Babylon. The two-year timeline is specific, measurable, and falsifiable — it will either happen or it will not. Hananiah's prophecy is testable in a way that Jeremiah's 70-year oracle is not (no living person in 594 BC would survive to verify the 70-year prediction). The short timeline gives Hananiah's message immediate political appeal and makes Jeremiah's long-term oracle seem less urgent.
4: I will also bring back to this place Jeconiah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, and all the exiles from Judah who went to Babylon, declares YHWH, for I will break the yoke of the king of Babylon. Hananiah extends the promise to include Jehoiachin and all the first-deportation exiles (597 BC). This is politically maximalist: not only the Temple vessels but the deported king and entire first exile community will return within two years. The promise taps directly into the hope of the exiles and their families, making it powerfully attractive.
5: Then the prophet Jeremiah spoke to Hananiah the prophet in the presence of the priests and all the people who were standing in the house of YHWH. The setting is public and formal — the Temple court with priests and people as witnesses. Jeremiah's response is not private consultation but a public counter-oracle in the same space where Hananiah spoke. The confrontation cannot be dismissed as a private theological dispute; it is a public crisis of prophetic authority.
6: And the prophet Jeremiah said, Amen! May YHWH do so; may YHWH make the words that you have prophesied come true, and bring back to this place from Babylon the vessels of the house of YHWH, and all the exiles. Jeremiah's response is startling: he says amen — may it be so! He does not immediately denounce Hananiah as a liar but expresses genuine desire that the restoration prophecy be true. The prophet who has been announcing judgment genuinely wishes the optimistic oracle were accurate. This models the complex pastoral reality: prophets who announce doom are not gleeful about it (cf. Jeremiah 20:17–18; Lamentations 3:1–20).
7: Yet hear now this word that I speak in your hearing and in the hearing of all the people. The qualifier follows the amen: despite his wish that Hananiah were right, Jeremiah must speak what he has received. The yet (akh) signals a critical qualification. The structure — wish it were true, but here is the reality — is the model of honest prophetic speech that neither pretends to wish disaster nor suppresses the accurate message.
8: The prophets who preceded you and me from ancient times prophesied war, famine, and pestilence against many countries and great kingdoms. Jeremiah invokes the prophetic tradition as a criterion: the historical pattern of genuine prophecy was primarily judgment-announcement. The prophets before them — Amos, Hosea, Micah, Isaiah — predominantly announced covenant consequences (war, famine, pestilence) rather than unconditional restoration. This statistical argument is not a denial that restoration prophecy is ever genuine, but an observation that the burden of proof lies with those who prophesy peace.
9: As for the prophet who prophesies peace, when the word of that prophet comes to pass, then it will be known that YHWH has truly sent that prophet. The Deuteronomic criterion of prophetic truth (Deuteronomy 18:21–22): fulfillment proves authenticity. The irony is that Hananiah's two-year prophecy will be precisely testable — within two years, the exiles will not have returned, the Temple vessels will still be in Babylon, and Jehoiachin will still be in prison. The fulfillment criterion cuts against Hananiah's short-term, falsifiable prediction far more than against Jeremiah's long-term oracle.
10: Then the prophet Hananiah took the yoke bar from the neck of Jeremiah the prophet and broke it. Hananiah performs a counter-sign-act: he takes the wooden yoke from Jeremiah's neck and breaks it. In sign-act logic, the physical action enacts the message — breaking the yoke symbolizes breaking Babylon's dominion. This is a direct challenge to Jeremiah's sign-act authority. The crowd watching would have seen Hananiah's dramatic action as a compelling visual claim: the yoke of Babylon will be broken just as this wooden bar is broken.
11: And Hananiah spoke in the presence of all the people, saying, Thus says YHWH: Even so will I break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon from the neck of all the nations within two years. But Jeremiah the prophet went his way. Hananiah doubles down on the two-year promise, now with the dramatic visual of the broken yoke. Jeremiah went his way — a remarkable sentence. Jeremiah does not counter-argue, does not produce a dramatic response, does not attempt to out-perform Hananiah's sign-act. He simply leaves. The prophet's authority does not require winning a public performance contest.
12: Sometime after the prophet Hananiah had broken the yoke bar from off the neck of Jeremiah the prophet, the word of YHWH came to Jeremiah. The divine word comes to Jeremiah after the confrontation, not during it — another mark of authentic prophecy versus performance. Genuine prophets do not manufacture responses on demand; they wait for the divine word to come and then speak it.
13: Go, tell Hananiah, Thus says YHWH: You have broken wooden bars, but you have made in their place bars of iron. The divine response to Hananiah's dramatic sign-act: the broken wooden yoke is replaced with iron. The counter-sign is more severe than the original. Encouraging resistance to Babylon with false prophecy will not produce freedom but a harder, more inescapable form of subjugation. The attempt to break YHWH's appointed instrument of judgment makes the judgment worse rather than averting it.
14: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: I have put upon the neck of all these nations an iron yoke to serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and they shall serve him, for I have given to him even the beasts of the field. The iron yoke reiterates and intensifies Jeremiah 27:6: YHWH has commissioned Nebuchadnezzar's dominion over nations and beasts. The wooden yoke that Hananiah broke was symbolic; the iron yoke YHWH establishes is the historical reality of Babylonian military dominance that no prophetic sign-act can break.
15: And Jeremiah the prophet said to the prophet Hananiah, Listen, Hananiah, YHWH has not sent you, and you have made this people trust in a lie. The direct confrontation Jeremiah avoided in vv6–11 arrives now with divine backing. YHWH has not sent you — the definitive criterion of prophetic invalidity. The harm Hananiah has done is specified: you have made this people trust in a lie (batach al-sheqer). False hope is not neutral; it actively prevents the repentance and surrender that might save lives. Hananiah's false prophecy is not merely wrong — it is an act of spiritual harm.
16: Therefore thus says YHWH: Behold, I will remove you from the face of the earth. This year you shall die, because you have uttered rebellion against YHWH. The death-oracle against Hananiah is specific and measurable: this year (hashanah hazot), not within two years but within one year. The charge is rebellion against YHWH (sarah dibbarta el-YHWH) — false prophecy is not mere error but an act of covenant treason. Speaking rebellion in YHWH's name is the crime of Deuteronomy 18:20.
17: In that same year, in the seventh month, the prophet Hananiah died. The brief, clinical fulfillment: Hananiah's death within the year proves Jeremiah's prophecy accurate and Hananiah's false. The narrative provides no theological commentary — it simply records the outcome. The reader draws the conclusion: the falsifiable prediction of the true prophet was fulfilled; the falsifiable prediction of the false prophet (two-year restoration) was not. Prophetic authentication by fulfillment operates precisely as Deuteronomy 18:21–22 described.
